title: Oxidation

infobox.value: This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Redox&action=edit&section=). *(April 2023)*

([/ˈrɛdɒks/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*RED-oks*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), [/ˈriːdɒks/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*REE-doks*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), **reduction–oxidation**[[2]](#cite_note-2) or **oxidation–reduction**[[3]](#cite_note-Petrucci2002-3): 150) is a type of [chemical reaction](/wiki/Chemical_reaction "Chemical reaction") in which the [oxidation states](/wiki/Oxidation_state "Oxidation state") of the [reactants](/wiki/Reactant "Reactant") change.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Oxidation is the loss of [electrons](/wiki/Electron "Electron") or an increase in the oxidation state, while reduction is the gain of electrons or a decrease in the oxidation state. The oxidation and reduction processes occur simultaneously in the chemical reaction.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Oxidation](/w/index.php?title=Oxidation&redirect=no "Oxidation"))

Chemical reaction with oxidation state changes

For other uses, see [Redox (disambiguation)](/wiki/Redox_(disambiguation) "Redox (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/NaF.gif/500px-NaF.gif)](/wiki/File:NaF.gif)

[Sodium](/wiki/Sodium "Sodium") "gives" one outer electron to [fluorine](/wiki/Fluorine "Fluorine"), bonding them to form [sodium fluoride](/wiki/Sodium_fluoride "Sodium fluoride"). The sodium atom is oxidized, and fluorine is reduced.



When a few drops of [glycerol](/wiki/Glycerol "Glycerol") (mild reducing agent) are added to powdered [potassium permanganate](/wiki/Potassium_permanganate "Potassium permanganate") (strong oxidizing agent), a violent redox reaction accompanied by self-ignition starts.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Redox_example.svg/250px-Redox_example.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Redox_example.svg)

Example of a reduction–oxidation reaction between sodium and chlorine, with the *OIL RIG* mnemonic[[1]](#cite_note-1)

**Redox** ([/ˈrɛdɒks/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*RED-oks*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), [/ˈriːdɒks/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*REE-doks*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), **reduction–oxidation**[[2]](#cite_note-2) or **oxidation–reduction**[[3]](#cite_note-Petrucci2002-3): 150) is a type of [chemical reaction](/wiki/Chemical_reaction "Chemical reaction") in which the [oxidation states](/wiki/Oxidation_state "Oxidation state") of the [reactants](/wiki/Reactant "Reactant") change.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Oxidation is the loss of [electrons](/wiki/Electron "Electron") or an increase in the oxidation state, while reduction is the gain of electrons or a decrease in the oxidation state. The oxidation and reduction processes occur simultaneously in the chemical reaction.

There are two classes of redox reactions:

* [Electron transfer](/wiki/Electron_transfer "Electron transfer") – Only one (usually) electron flows from the atom, ion, or molecule being oxidized to the atom, ion, or molecule that is reduced. This type of redox reaction is often discussed in terms of redox couples and electrode potentials.
* Atom transfer – An atom transfers from one [substrate](/wiki/Substrate_(chemistry) "Substrate (chemistry)") to another. For example, in the [rusting](/wiki/Rusting "Rusting") of [iron](/wiki/Iron "Iron"), the oxidation state of iron atoms increases as the iron converts to an [oxide](/wiki/Oxide "Oxide"), and simultaneously, the oxidation state of oxygen decreases as it accepts electrons released by the iron. Although oxidation reactions are commonly associated with forming oxides, other chemical species can serve the same function.[[5]](#cite_note-gale-5) In [hydrogenation](/wiki/Hydrogenation "Hydrogenation"), bonds like [C=C](/wiki/Alkene "Alkene") are reduced by [transfer of hydrogen atoms](/wiki/Transfer_hydrogenation "Transfer hydrogenation").

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Redox&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terminology")]

"Redox" is a [portmanteau](/wiki/Portmanteau "Portmanteau") of "reduction" and "oxidation". The term was first used in a 1928 article by [Leonor Michaelis](/wiki/Leonor_Michaelis "Leonor Michaelis") and [Louis B. Flexner](/wiki/Louis_B._Flexner "Louis B. Flexner").[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-i381-7)

Oxidation is a process in which a substance loses electrons. Reduction is a process in which a substance gains electrons.

The processes of oxidation and reduction occur simultaneously and cannot occur independently.[[5]](#cite_note-gale-5) In redox processes, the reductant transfers electrons to the oxidant. Thus, in the reaction, the reductant or [reducing agent](/wiki/Reducing_agent "Reducing agent") loses electrons and is oxidized, and the oxidant or [oxidizing agent](/wiki/Oxidizing_agent "Oxidizing agent") gains electrons and is reduced. The pair of an oxidizing and reducing agent that is involved in a particular reaction is called a redox pair. A redox couple is a reducing species and its corresponding oxidizing form,[[8]](#cite_note-8) e.g., [Fe2+](/wiki/Iron "Iron")/ [Fe3+](/wiki/Iron "Iron").The oxidation alone and the reduction alone are each called a *[half-reaction](/wiki/Half-reaction "Half-reaction")* because two half-reactions always occur together to form a whole reaction.[[5]](#cite_note-gale-5)

In [electrochemical reactions](/wiki/Electrochemistry "Electrochemistry") the oxidation and reduction processes do occur simultaneously but are separated in space.

### Oxidants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Redox&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Oxidants")]

Main article: [Oxidizing agent](/wiki/Oxidizing_agent "Oxidizing agent")

Oxidation originally implied a reaction with oxygen to form an oxide. Later, the term was expanded to encompass [substances](/wiki/Chemical_substance "Chemical substance") that accomplished chemical reactions similar to those of oxygen. Ultimately, the meaning was generalized to include all processes involving the loss of electrons or the increase in the oxidation state of a chemical species.[[9]](#cite_note-Petrucci2017-9): A49 Substances that have the ability to oxidize other substances (cause them to lose electrons) are said to be oxidative or oxidizing, and are known as oxidizing agents, oxidants, or oxidizers. The oxidant removes electrons from another substance, and is thus itself reduced.[[9]](#cite_note-Petrucci2017-9): A50 Because it "accepts" electrons, the oxidizing agent is also called an [electron acceptor](/wiki/Electron_acceptor "Electron acceptor"). Oxidants are usually chemical substances with elements in high oxidation states[[3]](#cite_note-Petrucci2002-3): 159 (e.g., [N  
2O  
4](/wiki/Dinitrogen_tetroxide "Dinitrogen tetroxide"), [MnO−  
4](/wiki/Permanganate "Permanganate"), [CrO  
3](/wiki/Chromium_trioxide "Chromium trioxide"), [Cr  
2O2−  
7](/wiki/Dichromate "Dichromate"), [OsO  
4](/wiki/Osmium(VIII)_oxide "Osmium(VIII) oxide")), or else highly [electronegative](/wiki/Electronegativity "Electronegativity") elements (e.g. [O2](/wiki/Oxygen "Oxygen"), [F2](/wiki/Fluorine "Fluorine"), [Cl2](/wiki/Chlorine "Chlorine"), [Br2](/wiki/Bromine "Bromine"), [I2](/wiki/Iodine "Iodine")) that can gain extra electrons by oxidizing another substance.[[3]](#cite_note-Petrucci2002-3): 909

Oxidizers are oxidants, but the term is mainly reserved for sources of oxygen, particularly in the context of explosions. [Nitric acid](/wiki/Nitric_acid "Nitric acid") is a strong oxidizer.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/GHS-pictogram-rondflam.svg/250px-GHS-pictogram-rondflam.svg.png)](/wiki/File:GHS-pictogram-rondflam.svg)

The [international](/wiki/Globally_Harmonized_System_of_Classification_and_Labeling_of_Chemicals "Globally Harmonized System of Classification and Labeling of Chemicals") [pictogram](/wiki/GHS_hazard_pictograms "GHS hazard pictograms") for oxidizing chemicals

### Reductants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Redox&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Reductants")]

Main article: [Reducing agent](/wiki/Reducing_agent "Reducing agent")

Substances that have the ability to reduce other substances (cause them to gain electrons) are said to be reductive or reducing and are known as reducing agents, reductants, or reducers. The reductant transfers electrons to another substance and is thus itself oxidized.[[3]](#cite_note-Petrucci2002-3): 159 Because it donates electrons, the reducing agent is also called an [electron donor](/wiki/Electron_donor "Electron donor"). Electron donors can also form [charge transfer complexes](/wiki/Charge_transfer_complex "Charge transfer complex") with electron acceptors. The word reduction originally referred to the loss in weight upon heating a metallic [ore](/wiki/Ore "Ore") such as a [metal oxide](/wiki/Metal_oxide "Metal oxide") to extract the metal. In other words, ore was "reduced" to metal.[[11]](#cite_note-Whitten-11) [Antoine Lavoisier](/wiki/Antoine_Lavoisier "Antoine Lavoisier") demonstrated that this loss of weight was due to the loss of oxygen as a gas. Later, scientists realized that the metal atom gains electrons in this process. The meaning of reduction then became generalized to include all processes involving a gain of electrons.[[11]](#cite_note-Whitten-11) Reducing equivalent refers to [chemical species](/wiki/Chemical_species "Chemical species") which transfer the equivalent of one [electron](/wiki/Electron "Electron") in redox reactions. The term is common in [biochemistry](/wiki/Biochemistry "Biochemistry").[[12]](#cite_note-12) A reducing equivalent can be an electron or a hydrogen atom as a [hydride ion](/wiki/Hydrogen_anion "Hydrogen anion").[[13]](#cite_note-Lehninger-2017-13)

Reductants in chemistry are very diverse. [Electropositive](/wiki/Electropositive "Electropositive") elemental [metals](/wiki/Metal "Metal"), such as [lithium](/wiki/Lithium "Lithium"), [sodium](/wiki/Sodium "Sodium"), [magnesium](/wiki/Magnesium "Magnesium"), [iron](/wiki/Iron "Iron"), [zinc](/wiki/Zinc "Zinc"), and [aluminium](/wiki/Aluminium "Aluminium"), are good reducing agents. These metals donate electrons relatively readily.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

[Hydride transfer reagents](/w/index.php?title=Hydride_transfer_reagents&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hydride transfer reagents (page does not exist)"), such as [NaBH4](/wiki/Sodium_borohydride "Sodium borohydride") and [LiAlH4](/wiki/Lithium_aluminium_hydride "Lithium aluminium hydride"), reduce by atom transfer: they transfer the equivalent of hydride or H−. These reagents are widely used in the reduction of [carbonyl](/wiki/Carbonyl "Carbonyl") compounds to [alcohols](/wiki/Alcohols "Alcohols").[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) A related method of reduction involves the use of hydrogen gas (H2) as sources of H atoms.[[3]](#cite_note-Petrucci2002-3): 288

### Electronation and de-electronation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Redox&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Electronation and de-electronation")]

The [electrochemist](/wiki/Electrochemistry "Electrochemistry") [John Bockris](/wiki/John_Bockris "John Bockris") proposed the words electronation and de-electronation to describe reduction and oxidation processes, respectively, when they occur at [electrodes](/wiki/Electrode "Electrode").[[17]](#cite_note-17) These words are analogous to [protonation](/wiki/Protonation "Protonation") and [deprotonation](/wiki/Deprotonation "Deprotonation").[[18]](#cite_note-18) [IUPAC](/wiki/IUPAC "IUPAC") has recognized the terms electronation[[19]](#cite_note-19) and de-electronation.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

## Rates, mechanisms, and energies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Redox&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Rates, mechanisms, and energies")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Redox&action=edit&section=). *(April 2023)* |

Redox reactions can occur slowly, as in the formation of [rust](/wiki/Rust "Rust"), or rapidly, as in the case of burning [fuel](/wiki/Fuel "Fuel"). Electron transfer reactions are generally fast, occurring within the time of mixing.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

The mechanisms of atom-transfer reactions are highly variable because many kinds of atoms can be transferred. Such reactions can also be quite complex, involving many steps. The mechanisms of electron-transfer reactions occur by two distinct pathways, [inner sphere electron transfer](/wiki/Inner_sphere_electron_transfer "Inner sphere electron transfer")[[22]](#cite_note-22) and [outer sphere electron transfer](/wiki/Outer_sphere_electron_transfer "Outer sphere electron transfer").[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Analysis of bond energies and [ionization energies](/wiki/Ionization_energy "Ionization energy") in water allows calculation of the thermodynamic aspects of redox reactions.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## Standard electrode potentials (reduction potentials)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Redox&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Standard electrode potentials (reduction potentials)")]

Each half-reaction has a standard [electrode potential](/wiki/Electrode_potential "Electrode potential") (*E*o  
cell), which is equal to the potential difference or [voltage](/wiki/Voltage "Voltage") at equilibrium under [standard conditions](/wiki/Standard_state "Standard state") of an [electrochemical cell](/wiki/Electrochemical_cell "Electrochemical cell") in which the [cathode](/wiki/Cathode "Cathode") reaction is the [half-reaction](/wiki/Half-reaction "Half-reaction") considered, and the [anode](/wiki/Anode "Anode") is a [standard hydrogen electrode](/wiki/Standard_hydrogen_electrode "Standard hydrogen electrode") where hydrogen is oxidized:[[25]](#cite_note-25)

:   1⁄2 H2 → H+ + e−

The electrode potential of each half-reaction is also known as its reduction potential (*E*o  
red), or potential when the half-reaction takes place at a cathode. The reduction potential is a measure of the tendency of the oxidizing agent to be reduced. Its value is zero for H+ + e− → 1⁄2H2 by definition, positive for oxidizing agents stronger than H+ (e.g., +2.866 V for F2) and negative for oxidizing agents that are weaker than H+ (e.g., −0.763V for Zn2+).[[9]](#cite_note-Petrucci2017-9): 873

For a redox reaction that takes place in a cell, the potential difference is:

:   *E*o  
    cell = *E*o  
    cathode − *E*o  
    anode

However, the potential of the reaction at the anode is sometimes expressed as an *oxidation potential*:

:   *E*o  
    ox = −*E*o  
    red

The oxidation potential is a measure of the tendency of the reducing agent to be oxidized but does not represent the physical potential at an electrode. With this notation, the cell voltage equation is written with a plus sign

:   *E*o  
    cell = *E*o  
    red(cathode) + *E*o  
    ox(anode)

## Examples of redox reactions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Redox&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Examples of redox reactions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/e/ee/Redox_reaction.png)](/wiki/File:Redox_reaction.png)

Illustration of a redox reaction

In the reaction between [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen") and [fluorine](/wiki/Fluorine "Fluorine"), hydrogen is being oxidized and fluorine is being reduced:

:   H2 + F2 → 2 HF

This spontaneous reaction releases a large amount of energy (542 kJ per 2 g of hydrogen) because two H-F bonds are much stronger than one H-H bond and one F-F bond.[[26]](#cite_note-26) This reaction can be analyzed as two [half-reactions](/wiki/Half-reaction "Half-reaction"). The oxidation reaction converts hydrogen to [protons](/wiki/Proton "Proton"):

:   H2 → 2 [H+](/wiki/Hydrogen_ion "Hydrogen ion") + 2 [e−](/wiki/Electron "Electron")

The reduction reaction converts [fluorine](/wiki/Fluorine "Fluorine") to the fluoride anion:

:   F2 + 2 e− → 2 [F−](/wiki/Fluoride "Fluoride")

The half-reactions are combined so that the electrons cancel:

:   |  |  |  |
    | --- | --- | --- |
    | H 2 | → | 2 H+ + 2 e− |
    | F 2 + 2 e− | → | 2 F− |
    | --- | | |
    | H2 + F2 | → | 2 H+ + 2 F− |

The protons and fluoride combine to form [hydrogen fluoride](/wiki/Hydrofluoric_acid "Hydrofluoric acid") in a non-redox reaction:

:   2 H+ + 2 F− → 2 HF

The overall reaction is:

:   H2 + F2 → 2 HF

### Metal displacement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Redox&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Metal displacement")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Galvanic_cell_with_no_cation_flow.svg/500px-Galvanic_cell_with_no_cation_flow.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Galvanic_cell_with_no_cation_flow.svg)

A redox reaction is the force behind an [electrochemical cell](/wiki/Electrochemical_cell "Electrochemical cell") like the [Galvanic cell](/wiki/Galvanic_cell "Galvanic cell") pictured. The battery is made out of a zinc electrode in a ZnSO4 solution connected with a wire and a porous disk to a copper electrode in a CuSO4 solution.

In this type of reaction, a [metal](/wiki/Metal "Metal") atom in a compound or solution is replaced by an atom of another metal. For example, [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper") is deposited when [zinc](/wiki/Zinc "Zinc") metal is placed in a [copper(II) sulfate](/wiki/Copper(II)_sulfate "Copper(II) sulfate") solution:

:   Zn(s) + CuSO4(aq) → ZnSO4(aq) + Cu(s)

In the above reaction, zinc metal displaces the copper(II) ion from the copper sulfate solution, thus liberating free copper metal. The reaction is spontaneous and releases 213 kJ per 65 g of zinc.

The ionic equation for this reaction is:

:   Zn + Cu2+ → Zn2+ + Cu

As two [half-reactions](/wiki/Half-reaction "Half-reaction"), it is seen that the zinc is oxidized:

:   Zn → Zn2+ + 2 e−

And the copper is reduced:

:   Cu2+ + 2 e− → Cu

### Other examples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Redox&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Other examples")]

* The reduction of [nitrate](/wiki/Nitrate "Nitrate") to [nitrogen](/wiki/Nitrogen "Nitrogen") in the presence of an acid ([denitrification](/wiki/Denitrification "Denitrification")):

:   :   2 NO−3 + 10 e− + 12 H+ → N2 + 6 H2O

* The [combustion](/wiki/Combustion "Combustion") of [hydrocarbons](/wiki/Hydrocarbon "Hydrocarbon"), such as in an [internal combustion engine](/wiki/Internal_combustion_engine "Internal combustion engine"), produces [water](/wiki/Water "Water"), [carbon dioxide](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide"), some partially oxidized forms such as [carbon monoxide](/wiki/Carbon_monoxide "Carbon monoxide"), and heat [energy](/wiki/Energy "Energy"). Complete oxidation of materials containing [carbon](/wiki/Carbon "Carbon") produces carbon dioxide.
* The stepwise oxidation of a hydrocarbon by oxygen, in [organic chemistry](/wiki/Organic_chemistry "Organic chemistry"), produces water and, successively: an [alcohol](/wiki/Alcohol_(chemistry) "Alcohol (chemistry)"), an [aldehyde](/wiki/Aldehyde "Aldehyde") or a [ketone](/wiki/Ketone "Ketone"), a [carboxylic acid](/wiki/Carboxylic_acid "Carboxylic acid"), and then a [peroxide](/wiki/Peroxide "Peroxide").

### Corrosion and rusting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Redox&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Corrosion and rusting")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Rust_screw.jpg/250px-Rust_screw.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rust_screw.jpg)

Oxides, such as [iron(III) oxide](/wiki/Iron(III)_oxide "Iron(III) oxide") or [rust](/wiki/Rust "Rust"), which consists of hydrated [iron(III) oxides](/wiki/Iron(III)_oxide "Iron(III) oxide") Fe2O3·*n*H2O and [iron(III) oxide-hydroxide](/wiki/Iron(III)_oxide-hydroxide "Iron(III) oxide-hydroxide") (FeO(OH), Fe(OH)3), form when oxygen combines with other elements.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/PyOx.JPG/250px-PyOx.JPG)](/wiki/File:PyOx.JPG)

Iron rusting in [pyrite](/wiki/Pyrite "Pyrite") cubes

* The term [corrosion](/wiki/Corrosion "Corrosion") refers to the electrochemical oxidation of metals in reaction with an oxidant such as oxygen. [Rusting](/wiki/Rust "Rust"), the formation of [iron oxides](/wiki/Iron_oxide "Iron oxide"), is a well-known example of electrochemical corrosion: it forms as a result of the oxidation of [iron](/wiki/Iron "Iron") metal. Common rust often refers to [iron(III) oxide](/wiki/Iron(III)_oxide "Iron(III) oxide"), formed in the following chemical reaction:

:   :   4 Fe + 3 O2 → 2 Fe2O3

* The oxidation of iron(II) to iron(III) by [hydrogen peroxide](/wiki/Hydrogen_peroxide "Hydrogen peroxide") in the presence of an [acid](/wiki/Acid "Acid"):

:   :   Fe2+ → Fe3+ + e−
    :   H2O2 + 2 e− → 2 OH−
:   Here the overall equation involves adding the reduction equation to twice the oxidation equation, so that the electrons cancel:

    :   2 Fe2+ + H2O2 + 2 H+ → 2 Fe3+ + 2 H2O

### Disproportionation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Redox&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Disproportionation")]

A [disproportionation](/wiki/Disproportionation "Disproportionation") reaction is one in which a single substance is both oxidized and reduced. For example, [thiosulfate](/wiki/Thiosulfate "Thiosulfate") ion with sulfur in oxidation state +2 can react in the presence of acid to form elemental sulfur (oxidation state 0) and [sulfur dioxide](/wiki/Sulfur_dioxide "Sulfur dioxide") (oxidation state +4).

:   S2O2−3 + 2 H+ → S + SO2 + H2O

Thus one sulfur atom is reduced from +2 to 0, while the other is oxidized from +2 to +4.[[9]](#cite_note-Petrucci2017-9): 176

## Redox reactions in industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Redox&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Redox reactions in industry")]

[Cathodic protection](/wiki/Cathodic_protection "Cathodic protection") is a technique used to control the corrosion of a metal surface by making it the [cathode](/wiki/Cathode "Cathode") of an [electrochemical cell](/wiki/Electrochemical_cell "Electrochemical cell"). A simple method of protection connects protected metal to a more easily corroded "[sacrificial anode](/wiki/Sacrificial_anode "Sacrificial anode")" to act as the [anode](/wiki/Anode "Anode"). The sacrificial metal, instead of the protected metal, then corrodes.

Oxidation is used in a wide variety of industries, such as in the production of [cleaning products](/wiki/Category:Cleaning_products "Category:Cleaning products") and oxidizing [ammonia](/wiki/Ammonia "Ammonia") to produce [nitric acid](/wiki/Nitric_acid "Nitric acid").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Redox reactions are the foundation of electrochemical cells, which can generate electrical energy or support [electrosynthesis](/wiki/Electrosynthesis "Electrosynthesis"). Metal [ores](/wiki/Ore "Ore") often contain metals in oxidized states, such as oxides or sulfides, from which the pure metals are extracted by [smelting](/wiki/Smelting "Smelting") at high temperatures in the presence of a reducing agent. The process of [electroplating](/wiki/Electroplating "Electroplating") uses redox reactions to coat objects with a thin layer of a material, as in [chrome-plated](/wiki/Chrome_plating "Chrome plating") [automotive](/wiki/Automotive "Automotive") parts, [silver plating](/wiki/Plating#Silver_plating "Plating") [cutlery](/wiki/Cutlery "Cutlery"), [galvanization](/wiki/Galvanization "Galvanization") and [gold-plated](/wiki/Gold-plated "Gold-plated") [jewelry](/wiki/Jewelry "Jewelry").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Many essential [biological](/wiki/Biology "Biology") processes involve redox reactions. Before some of these processes can begin, iron must be [assimilated](/wiki/Assimilation_(biology) "Assimilation (biology)") from the environment.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

[Aerobic cellular respiration](/wiki/Cellular_respiration "Cellular respiration"), for instance, is the oxidation of substrates [in this case: [glucose](/wiki/Glucose "Glucose") (C6H12O6)] and the reduction of [oxygen](/wiki/Oxygen "Oxygen") to [water](/wiki/Water "Water"). The summary equation for aerobic respiration is:

:   C6H12O6 + 6 O2 → 6 CO2 + 6 H2O + Energy[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The process of cellular respiration also depends heavily on the reduction of [NAD+](/wiki/Nicotinamide_adenine_dinucleotide "Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide") to NADH and the reverse reaction (the oxidation of NADH to NAD+). [Photosynthesis](/wiki/Photosynthesis "Photosynthesis") and cellular respiration are complementary, but photosynthesis is not the reverse of the redox reaction in cellular respiration:

:   6 CO2 + 6 H2O + [light energy](/wiki/Photon "Photon") → C6H12O6 + 6 O2

[Biological energy](/wiki/Biological_energy "Biological energy") is frequently stored and released using redox reactions. Photosynthesis involves the reduction of [carbon dioxide](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide") into [sugars](/wiki/Sugar "Sugar") and the oxidation of [water](/wiki/Water_(molecule) "Water (molecule)") into molecular oxygen. The reverse reaction, respiration, oxidizes sugars to produce carbon dioxide and water. As intermediate steps, the reduced carbon compounds are used to reduce [nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide](/wiki/Nicotinamide_adenine_dinucleotide "Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide") (NAD+) to NADH, which then contributes to the creation of a [proton gradient](/wiki/Proton_gradient "Proton gradient"), which drives the synthesis of [adenosine triphosphate](/wiki/Adenosine_triphosphate "Adenosine triphosphate") (ATP) and is maintained by the reduction of oxygen. In animal cells, [mitochondria](/wiki/Mitochondria "Mitochondria") perform similar functions.

See also: [Membrane potential](/wiki/Membrane_potential "Membrane potential")

The term *redox state* is often used to describe the balance of [GSH/GSSG](/wiki/Glutathione "Glutathione"), NAD+/NADH and [NADP+/NADPH](/wiki/Nicotinamide_adenine_dinucleotide_phosphate "Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate") in a biological system such as a [cell](/wiki/Cell_(biology) "Cell (biology)") or [organ](/wiki/Organ_(biology) "Organ (biology)"). The redox state is reflected in the balance of several sets of metabolites (e.g., [lactate](/wiki/Lactic_acid "Lactic acid") and [pyruvate](/wiki/Pyruvate "Pyruvate"), [beta-hydroxybutyrate](/wiki/Beta-hydroxybutyrate "Beta-hydroxybutyrate") and [acetoacetate](/wiki/Acetoacetate "Acetoacetate")), whose interconversion is dependent on these ratios. Redox mechanisms also control some cellular processes. Redox proteins and their genes must be co-located for redox regulation according to the [CoRR hypothesis](/wiki/CoRR_hypothesis "CoRR hypothesis") for the function of [DNA](/wiki/DNA "DNA") in [mitochondria](/wiki/Mitochondrion "Mitochondrion") and [chloroplasts](/wiki/Chloroplast "Chloroplast").

### Redox cycling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Redox&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Redox cycling")]

Wide varieties of [aromatic compounds](/wiki/Aromaticity "Aromaticity") are [enzymatically](/wiki/Enzyme "Enzyme") reduced to form [free radicals](/wiki/Radical_(chemistry) "Radical (chemistry)") that contain one more electron than their parent compounds. In general, the electron donor is any of a wide variety of [flavoenzymes](/wiki/Flavoenzyme "Flavoenzyme") and their [coenzymes](/wiki/Coenzyme "Coenzyme"). Once formed, these anion free radicals reduce molecular oxygen to [superoxide](/wiki/Superoxide "Superoxide") and regenerate the unchanged parent compound. The net reaction is the oxidation of the flavoenzyme's coenzymes and the reduction of molecular oxygen to form superoxide. This catalytic behavior has been described as a [futile cycle](/wiki/Futile_cycle "Futile cycle") or redox cycling.

## Redox reactions in geology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Redox&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Redox reactions in geology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/VysokePece1.jpg/250px-VysokePece1.jpg)](/wiki/File:VysokePece1.jpg)

[Blast furnaces](/wiki/Blast_furnace "Blast furnace") of [Třinec Iron and Steel Works](/wiki/T%C5%99inec_Iron_and_Steel_Works "Třinec Iron and Steel Works"), Czech Republic

Minerals are generally oxidized derivatives of metals. [Iron](/wiki/Iron "Iron") is mined as ores such as [magnetite](/wiki/Magnetite "Magnetite") (Fe3O4) and [hematite](/wiki/Hematite "Hematite") (Fe2O3). [Titanium](/wiki/Titanium "Titanium") is mined as its dioxide, usually in the form of [rutile](/wiki/Rutile "Rutile") (TiO2). These oxides must be reduced to obtain the corresponding metals, often achieved by heating these oxides with carbon or carbon monoxide as reducing agents. [Blast furnaces](/wiki/Blast_furnace "Blast furnace") are the reactors where iron oxides and coke (a form of carbon) are combined to produce molten iron. The main chemical reaction producing the molten iron is:[[28]](#cite_note-28)

:   Fe2O3 + 3 CO → 2 Fe + 3 CO2

## Redox reactions in soils

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Redox&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Redox reactions in soils")]

[Electron transfer](/wiki/Electron_transfer "Electron transfer") reactions are central to myriad processes and properties in soils, and [redox potential](/wiki/Redox_potential "Redox potential"), quantified as Eh (platinum electrode potential ([voltage](/wiki/Voltage "Voltage")) relative to the standard hydrogen electrode) or pe (analogous to pH as −log electron activity), is a master variable, along with pH, that controls and is governed by chemical reactions and biological processes. Early theoretical research with applications to flooded soils and [paddy rice](/wiki/Paddy_rice "Paddy rice") production was seminal for subsequent work on thermodynamic aspects of redox and plant root growth in soils.[[29]](#cite_note-29) Later work built on this foundation, and expanded it for understanding redox reactions related to heavy metal oxidation state changes, [pedogenesis](/wiki/Pedogenesis "Pedogenesis") and morphology, organic compound degradation and formation, [free radical](/wiki/Free_radical "Free radical") chemistry, [wetland](/wiki/Wetland "Wetland") delineation, [soil remediation](/wiki/Soil_remediation "Soil remediation"), and various methodological approaches for characterizing the redox status of soils.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)

## Mnemonics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Redox&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Mnemonics")]

Main article: [List of chemistry mnemonics](/wiki/List_of_chemistry_mnemonics "List of chemistry mnemonics")

The key terms involved in redox can be confusing.[[32]](#cite_note-Robertson-32)[[33]](#cite_note-Chemistry_ConceptsAndApplications-33) For example, a reagent that is oxidized loses electrons; however, that reagent is referred to as the reducing agent. Likewise, a reagent that is reduced gains electrons and is referred to as the oxidizing agent.[[34]](#cite_note-Rodgers-34) These [mnemonics](/wiki/Mnemonics "Mnemonics") are commonly used by students to help memorise the terminology:[[35]](#cite_note-Zumhahl-35)

* "[OIL RIG](/wiki/Oil_rig "Oil rig")" — **o**xidation **i**s **l**oss of electrons, **r**eduction **i**s **g**ain of electrons[[32]](#cite_note-Robertson-32)[[33]](#cite_note-Chemistry_ConceptsAndApplications-33)[[34]](#cite_note-Rodgers-34)[[35]](#cite_note-Zumhahl-35)
* "LEO the lion says GER [grr]" — **l**oss of **e**lectrons is **o**xidation, **g**ain of **e**lectrons is **r**eduction[[32]](#cite_note-Robertson-32)[[33]](#cite_note-Chemistry_ConceptsAndApplications-33)[[34]](#cite_note-Rodgers-34)[[35]](#cite_note-Zumhahl-35)
* "LEORA says GEROA" — the loss of electrons is called oxidation (reducing agent); the gain of electrons is called reduction (oxidizing agent).[[34]](#cite_note-Rodgers-34)
* "RED CAT" and "AN OX", or "AnOx RedCat" ("an ox-red cat") — reduction occurs at the cathode and the anode is for oxidation
* "RED CAT gains what AN OX loses" – reduction at the cathode gains (electrons) what anode oxidation loses (electrons)
* "PANIC" – Positive Anode and Negative is Cathode. This applies to [electrolytic cells](/wiki/Electrolytic_cell "Electrolytic cell") which release stored electricity, and can be recharged with electricity. PANIC does not apply to cells that can be recharged with redox materials. These [galvanic or voltaic cells](/wiki/Galvanic_cell "Galvanic cell"), such as [fuel cells](/wiki/Fuel_cell "Fuel cell"), produce electricity from internal redox reactions. Here, the positive electrode is the cathode and the negative is the anode.
